metric_value: 'Interference in urban Wi-Fi nets is close at hand. The more powerful or advanced flavors of 802.11 wont be a help to users, because they are automatically dumbed down by other access points.' — Kastner
notes: Core thesis of 'Urban Wi-Fi Crash of 2004' report

metric_value: 'New purchases are going to 802.11g because of the improved throughput, but theres absolutely no improvement in the interface.' — Kastner
Report title: 'The Urban Wi-Fi Crash of 2004.' Predicts urban-hot-spot interference will threaten users as overlapping access points multiply in 'the shadows'.
notes: Dated headline; interference problem real but crash averted

metric_name: Did the predicted 2004 Wi-Fi crash occur
metric_value: No — crash averted by rapid 802.11n (2007-2009) adoption, widespread 5GHz migration, improved rate-adaptation, and mesh/enterprise-controller architectures. But interference issues did become real and sustained in 2.4GHz by 2007-2010. Partial validation.
notes: Problem correct, timing and severity overstated

metric_value: Aberdeen Group report titled 'The Urban Wi-Fi Crash of 2004' by Peter Kastner

Kastner's framing implicitly validated 5GHz as escape path — 802.11a never mass-adopted standalone, but 802.11n/ac/ax all leveraged 5GHz to escape 2.4GHz congestion, culminating in Wi-Fi 6E/7 (6GHz) in 2020s.
